title: When an Error Happens: Disclosure, Incident Reporting, and Just Culture
subtitle: A patient mistakenly received a double dose of her beta-blocker. She is stable but mildly bradycardic. The nurse must protect the patient, disclose the error honestly, document factually, and report it through a just-culture system.

patient.name: Beatrice Holloway
patient.admitDate: Medical unit. Received a duplicate dose of scheduled metoprolol due to a process breakdown, recognized shortly after. Currently asymptomatic with mild bradycardia and borderline blood pressure; on a cardiac monitor. Provider notified.

vitals.temp: Next dose held per order; monitoring

nursesNote: Beatrice Holloway, 70, received a duplicate dose of metoprolol from a process breakdown, caught shortly after; asymptomatic with mild bradycardia, on telemetry, provider notified and next dose held. Priorities: patient safety FIRST (assess, intervene, monitor), then honest disclosure of the error to the patient (team-led, factual, non-blaming), objective charting of the clinical facts/notifications/interventions (without the words 'incident report' or blame), completion of an occurrence report for quality improvement, and participation in a just-culture root-cause analysis focused on fixing the system rather than punishing an honest mistake.
providerOrders: Assess and monitor the patient for effects of the medication error (telemetry)
Notify the provider; carry out corrective orders (hold next dose)
Disclose the error to the patient honestly (team-led)
Document clinical facts objectively; do not reference the incident report in the chart
Complete the occurrence report; participate in root-cause analysis (just culture)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What should guide the response to an error?

opts.text: After an error, the patient's safety and clinical needs come first
Patients have the right to be informed of errors that affect them
An incident/occurrence report is completed for quality improvement
The incident report is not part of the medical record
The best response to an error is to conceal it
Just culture encourages reporting errors and near-misses to improve safety
First assess the patient, intervene, notify the provider, and monitor
Disclosure should be honest, factual, and non-blaming, usually team-led
Chart the clinical facts objectively, without blame or the words "incident report"
Just culture separates human error (console) from reckless behavior (discipline)
Charting "incident report filed" in the record is recommended
Root-cause analysis focuses on system fixes, not individual blame

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response to the error

opts.text: Assess the patient and provide any needed clinical intervention
Notify the provider and monitor the patient for harm
Participate in honest disclosure to the patient/family
Document the clinical facts objectively in the chart
Complete the incident report and join the system review

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Appropriate or inappropriate action after an error?

opts.text: Assess the patient and notify the provider immediately
Monitor the patient for effects of the error
Disclose the error honestly to the patient/family
Complete an objective incident/occurrence report
Chart the clinical facts, assessment, notification, and interventions
Participate in the root-cause analysis to fix the system
Hide the error and hope no harm occurs
Document "incident report filed" in the medical record
opts.rat: Keep it out of the chart.

opts.text: Alter the chart to conceal what happened

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Ensure the patient is safe and stable first
Notify the provider and carry out corrective orders
Support truthful disclosure and answer questions honestly
Chart objective clinical facts and the notifications/interventions
File the occurrence report and contribute to the root-cause analysis

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.sub: Supports or undermines a safety culture?

opts.text: The error is reported so the system can be improved
Near-misses are reported without fear of punishment
The patient is told honestly and is monitored
A root-cause analysis targets the system, not one person
Staff hide errors to avoid blame
A nurse is punished for an honest, system-driven mistake with no learning
The chart is falsified to cover the error
The patient is never informed of an error that affected her

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
